channel.title: Comments for page "Re: Promotion"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Re: Promotion"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliant story, and it all contains a special kind of "[That's] fucked up". Up until Misty revealed that everyone was faking it (save for -D), it seemed like that while something was wrong, it didn't seem clear what that was. Most SCPs tend to suffer a bit when you reveal more information about their background, but this is one of those rare cases where it actually adds a lot of benefit.

Shame I can't do better than +1, but it's something I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddammit, why'd you have to go and make me feel like that?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cain and Able are the two sons of Adam that -D refers to in his last interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Joining the club of people who logged in after being logged out for years solely to +1 this article and all SCP-2422 articles. The tale just really did it for me, every emotion that I was supposed to feel was felt and I am now really wishing someone would write another tale that gives this story a conclusion. Just who is the wandering father? Since the family that represents the SCP were not originally a family (correct?) and now those two doctors know about two humans who went missing some time ago, they have a big clue on where to start searching.

What scares the the most is the thought that people like the wandering father actually exists in real life… People who capture other people to torture them as opposed to declare a kidnapping to the family and demand money are a real thing…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sincerely hate you for forcing me to log into the bloody site in order to upvote this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My god, this is dark.
you got my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This tale is so expertly written. Each tab slowly increases tension to the big climax of the story, and this improves 2422 significantly. I love it. Also, congratulations on getting February front-page featured!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really fucked up in a really good way. Got upvotes from me both for this and for the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was fun to read and had very good pacing. I do agree with Kalinin's assessment that the Foundation seems fairly incompetent in this supplement. The interview with -D also felt narmy and could be rewritten to have the same effect while inducing less cringe. But overall I enjoyed this a lot, so, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I've literally just signed in after two years just to give this quite brilliant tale a thumbs up. Excellent work and very well put together with a clean slow build up to the dramatic reveal.

If I had any criticism it's that the Foundation seems to have gone softer than butter after spending an hour in the simmering sun of Death Valley. Two employees receiving extended leave to mentally recoup themselves after a mildly distressing interview, what a bunch of prissys. Somebody should get Clef in here to teach these children how to never show emotion. One trip down that hallway ought to do it ("All they want is the blood").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have never liked a tale so much or seen one so well written since qntm's antimemetic stories. A couple of spelling mistakes, but I am easily able to overlook them. I seriously wish I could upvote this twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really good! All of these logs and back-story (not what I'd expected the origins to be) pushed me to enthusiastically like this and the SCP article itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I assume Abe stole from The Serpent's Hand and aped the name of the library they visit, I'm not sure what Cain and Able have to do with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. Brilliant story, I loved it. +1. Have you made anything else like this? I'd like to see more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. This is an entirely perfect example of why you read the supplements. The original article wasn't anything that stood out, but this was one of those rare gems that I'll reliably remember out of the mass of scips I've read.

Good show with the humanization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of the issues I have with SCP-2422 are present here, and there's no need to repeat a lot of it.

The reactions of the researchers in this story to the individual family members, especially prior to the autistic kid doing his thing, seem way out of proportion to the actual human trauma that a professional therapist is likely to have encountered. I can buy that a psychiatric professional might be shocked by the story of someone being kidnapped and experimented on by a killer in a basement. Okay. Prior to that, however, none of the things being related to the researchers are really out of the realm of everyday experience for someone whose job is to listen and deal with this sort of thing. They all seem a little naive for people working for a shadow government organization that specializes in extrajudicial detention.

In fact, there seems to be a general attitude of unprofessionalism in this facility. The staff are dating each other, and then allowing themselves to do stuff like serve as a therapist to a person that they have an active dislike of, resulting in a highly inappropriate and personal interview with a subject in the "Re: Love" tab. I understand these things happen in supposedly professional environments. However, the basic unfamiliarity with common sense on display in some of these interactions lends itself to a general "Incompetent Foundation" interpretation here. That's not something I can really hold with from a basic logic point of view. An Incompetent Foundation would have ended the world eighteen times by now.

On another point, the entirety of the kid's meltdown just didn't work for me. The business about God felt narmy as hell, and similar to the Poor Girl in Containment thing from the source article, I am profoundly tired of the thing in containment lecturing the Foundation about how evil/misguided/whatever it is. That angle has never really resonated with me, and too often it becomes the vehicle for some sort of "science as destroyer of mystery and wonder" vision, which I could write several dozen more pages about in opposition.

This concept, in both article and tale formats, lacks to my reading any feeling of authenticity or deeper meaning. Certainly, not every article needs to bring some grand new insight about the human condition. But your aim is to get at something deeper, and in that these pieces seem to me to be missing the mark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jumping in after being offline a week to say I love this. The writing and human drama are absolutely engrossing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that's because the Wandering Father has enough information about the Foundation as to know which SCP number the family would receive beforehand . It should also alarm the Foundation personnel about information leaks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. One thing though;

Though -C is telling a story, why would the Wandering Father call her SCP-2422-C in her story? Would not the Wandering Father have something to call her before dropping her off and sending them on their way to Site-17?

I love it otherwise, but that little bit is the only thing I don't get. +1 otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I first read the article, I thought it was pretty good. Now that I've read this… I think it's great. Very nicely developed, the logs are attention-holding and provide a good progression, as well as revealing some very interesting tidbits about the family and their origins. I definitely was not expecting these implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was exactly my intention and reasoning (both in the tale and in the article itself), except in my mind, the Foundation going along with it was less of a "why not" than a "since we know nothing about these people, less friction is better until we can figure out what they're capable of" on their part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the main article under acquisitions, it says that when they came to the gate, they refered to themselves as their numerical designation. So I think that is the author of this tales way of going about that. I mean, if you have an empty slot and someone says "hey, I'm this slot!" Then why not? Although the foundation could just screw with them and put them in a new number… Hard to say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A great read, well written. A whole slew of emotions captured very proficiently. I thought that 2422 was pretty bland but I now feel differently.

My only criticism is that it seems odd that Abe gave them their SCP designations. Even if he's Foundation, I feel it would be unlikely that he would accurately foresee the precise designation they would end up being given, unless I'm missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really needs to be linked from the 2422 article somehow because it is fantastic and fucking creepy and very well-written.

There are a few typos, though, some of which I'm going to fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very rarely does it add something to explain where the anomalous effects of a SCP come from, or just there origins in general, and thats neither bad or good but just how its done. This is what I'd consider a very rare but shining example of a great origin story for a SCP. Great work, couple grammar mistakes, but nobody's perfect. +1 cuz You da man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess this lies in the loose ends from SCP-2422 properly. Good job on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic. Over Thinking, I don't know what else you've contributed to the site, but know that I expect great things from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much!

Considering 2422-D appears to be autistic to those without this information, I would assume without his anomalous properties on his side he would just shut down and become noncommunicative. It's interesting to think how he would react outside of that, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 Loved the tale and how you handled it with the multiple email and writing form.
Would be nice to see SCP-2422-D decommissioning… maybe a Clef interview(due to his anti bender abilities)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much! I'm glad you like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just got better and better with every tab. I wasn't even that big on 2422, but this almost changes my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much!

I've moved the information into the discussion. I originally intended for the email to be the hub and to have each document on a separate page, which is why it was there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the authors notes at the end belong in the discussion, they kind of brought me out of the tale. I like it, but it didn't light my world on fire. Neutral vote for now, may upvote upon a reread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Just wow. Adds so much to SCP-2422. Nice slow reveal, each tab ups the tension just a touch. By the time I got to the main reveal, I was twitching for it.

Now I just want to know what he stole from who.

+1. Excellent tale!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Information (possible spoilers)
Information

Articles referenced in this tale:

SCP-2422 by OverThinking
SCP-2240 by Mdnthrvst
The Serpent's Hand
SCP-076 by Kain Pathos Crow
SCP-073 by Kain Pathos Crow

Backstory:

SCP-2422 was created by a mysterious entity known as Wandering Father, created to exist within Site-17 and bring Joy to its occupants. Not much else is known about their past.

These few classified interview logs – passed down to an incoming Project Director by his predecessor – reveal their origins and their story through their interactions with each other, their containment, and the various researchers who work with them.

There is always more than meets the eye.

Special Thanks:

Thank you to Doctor Cimmerian and EldritchCyanide for critiquing this in the Forums, to Zyn for calling attention to it in chat, and to Decibelles for informing me of the actual author of 073 and 076.
